Well, you know, anybody that died in this country when I was a kid, they never bought a casket. They'd go over there and get one. They made real nice ones. Well, they had to pay him for them. He sold them. Yeah, he sold them. That was his business. They wouldn't stay in one kind. They'd make, you know, different kinds all the time. Well, they'd like to make a different design. words: By the time they got there, you mean?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They were there to help her, but she said, no, I didn't take care of her. She did. Now, that was that tall, skinny woman that ...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know they were there? She's still delivered her own baby?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yes, she did. Of course, they were right there to take care of it afterwards, you know. But she said, no, I can take care of her. She did. Now, that's an incident you don't hear of very often, unless it has to be, you know, but she did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his name?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did I see that picture? Did we have it at home? Yeah, we had it at home, didn't we, at Hampton?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, I think so. There was a picture in the trees grew tall. We were looking at it not long ago. Her husband. When I was here before, we saw it in the book. You pointed it out to me. Yeah, I was in here. But he wasn't well. I don't know why. What was wrong. But he was heavy, but he just wasn't well. So she had to take all of her work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people give up and move out, leave, in the years that you folks were growing up?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, some of us, like the bachelors, that would have homesteads, they'd sell and move out, you know. Or they'd go out and sell, come back and sell. And, well, a lot of them did. And then, of course, Bogle began to build up after the railroad went through, and then it was more jobs kept coming in, you know, so they could make a fair living. This is the family. This is the one she delivered herself, right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think of a deal like that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: It's all right if you're capable of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was, like people around here, were they more attached to Deary or Bogle as a town?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I think this town was more attached to Deary. Well, it wasn't quite as far to travel. And then, of course, naturally, the snow was out of the roads this way quicker than it was the other way, you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: The roads were better down this way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there was pretty bad times, too, wasn't there? Which times are you referring to? Pardon? What times are you referring to? Well, that was in earlier days, you know, before the roads were graveled or even, it wasn't even a highway, was it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No. It was just, you know, they'd be just mud holes, and you'd have to go through them and get through them the best way you could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We've got out of them many times, haven't we, Mae? You didn't hear me? Start to Deary and get stuck?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I guess so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did the road go? Was it more or less where it is now?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, no, right down by here. Right down by here and turn the corner right here. The corner and then it went down through this way and on the backside of Halcyon. And then right on in there by the Anderson Place and right into Deary there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever walk there from here, or did you always take it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I don't know. They had them little stores here, and we made our clothes out of, a lot of times, our clothes were made out of flour sacks and sugar sacks. Now that sounds terrible, but it was. They were bleached and taken care of and made good. That is our underwear. And that's the way we lived. We didn't have to go to town, because we'd get out of what little grocers we needed here. I don't know when we ever went to town.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I sold it there at the little store they had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean his folks?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, I was thinking of yours. You had a little store. No, he had just a general grocery store. Just like any little store would have. You have almost everything like that. That is just staple goods, because we didn't buy fancy canned goods like we do now. But, uh, corn and beans and just staple stuff. And meat, of course. He just had an ordinary store. And it had a little dry, a few dry goods, not much. Like the lumberjacks colts and socks. But his dad had a bigger store. He carried quite a lot of the men's wear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At Helmer or at Helmer or Grangeville?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, here. After he moved up here to the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't have anything like that down there, did you? Did you have clothing for man? He just moved his stock from down there up here, Floyd, or was that a deal?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He moved it up here. He just took the store and moved it.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They left that place down where the store was and they built a new home up on the hill. And when they moved the new home up on the hill, they put him in the store up here. He had a pretty fair store. A lot bigger than my dad's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you two meet?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: You and... Oh, we met in about 1970. Going to school. We had to walk to the old log schoolhouse. About a mile from here up there. He'd walk in one side of the room and I'd walk in the other. That's where he met.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Now we're at school together. Well, we've locked each other, I guess. Well, hi there, Larry. Like a working man. Oh, very hard. You look like you worked anyway.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Maybe even less. When his father gave her food and not really thinking he'd get paid back for it anymore. Was there much charity like that when you'd... Well, he didn't give it to him. He sold it to them. You see, he had to run his store on a basis, you know. So if he didn't get the money back in, it was hard for him to get the food back in for them for these people, see. And he could let each one of them have so much, a credit. Then he'd have to cut them off, you see. And that made it awful hard, too. And that's what happened to this lady when she came down. He'd just let so many different ones have credit, you know. So it made it hard on the people that had little stores as well as the homesteaders, but they'd have to work around some way and get them to pay these bills, and then they could go on, you know. But they'd carry them so long so they could live. And, of course, when they could prove up on their places, then they had money, you know, enough that they could pay their... ...expenses and bills.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did they have money just because they proved up?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Then they'd sell their timber and then they... They would probably just sell the whole thing, you know, whatever, their whole homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what most folks did once they proved up on those homesteads, like around McGarry Butte?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They just sold them right away. Not too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't hold them too long, did they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They had got a chance to sell us both when the potlatch come in and then they'd sell, you know. Then they'd go other places, or they'd move in the bowl or somewhere up in here, maybe, if it was a vacant house. And then they'd go back east.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They don't hear from there, do they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Or go to work for the company, I imagine. Well, wherever they could get work, you know. And after they would come in, of course, the jobs opened up, you know, because there's a little logging here and there. But a lot of those other homesteads, like on the ridges, they just wanted to hold on to them. They didn't sell them once they proved up and they went to farming. You know, not all of them. Some of them sold, though. And then, of course, whoever would buy would improve the land and build them up, you know. And that's the way the farms got started down around on the ridge. And it was good soil, I don't know, you see. And when one would buy and find it was good, then maybe their neighbor would come from the east or somewhere and buy. But most of the bowls and a few of them stayed, but there was an awful lot of them moved out and sold. But, of course, like I said, the people that come in then would improve the end and plow more fields. And then, of course, that was in team days. And then, of course, after the tractors were then really, really went to town. But the ridge is good soil.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Your father, he carried people, too, on credit. Well, he could, yes. But he didn't have the capital to carry, you know, to be able to carry them too long. And they would, you know, like all stores, they'd lose some now and then. People that just didn't want to pay and didn't, but he didn't keep his store too long.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would those people, would people who wouldn't pay still stay in the country, or would they have to get out if they didn't pay?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, it just had to... Were you able to collect, is what I mean. Well, no. There was no way of hardly to collect, because in them days they didn't have steady jobs. And some of them that didn't pay didn't work. They'd fish and hunt and live off of the others. We had one family here that everybody couldn't hear a captain. He could have worked, but they'd feel sorry for the family and everybody'd give them fruit and vegetables. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the husband drink or was he just lazy?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, he just didn't work. Just depended on the community to take care of him. But people did that. I mean, they didn't try to kick him out. You'd find a few that worked, but most of them was good on the... No, what I mean is that the people, I mean, the community let the family stay. They didn't... Oh, yes. Well, they had a little place of their own to stay, and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't seem like they didn't rent much, did they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They'd build little shacks. More. Washing off in the rain. Yeah. They'd build little shacks somewhere and live in it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it feel sorry for the wife and kids? Was that it, or something?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I can't imagine you could have too much respect for, you know... Well, everybody had more respect for people. They thought more of their neighbors, you know. I think now there's so many you can't do it. And they're here and there and gone, and there's ways of making livings, you know, now. And then there wasn't. It was hard. Well, the people who live here are a lot closer to each other than they are now. It was just a different feeling. Everybody kind of... well, kind of looked after the other one. They're building now. I'm just experimenting. Oh. But this family that you mentioned here, it didn't work at all. Oh, he'd go out and buy cattle and, you know, maybe for a little while or something like that. But he just never made enough to get along on, and then he'd maybe lay around for months and months. Oh, yeah. Let me see if you all know, too. They'd come and ask if they could get some fruit or if somebody had some eggs or potatoes they could borrow. And then he'd go out and work a little while, and they'd get a little grubby and, you know.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He wasn't very well liked. I never found him yet. Yeah, that's what I was thinking. I can't imagine... I wouldn't give a thing if he'd done that. No, that's okay. That doesn't matter to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you find them in other communities or just in a homestead community or small towns?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I guess what I'm more interested in is the attitude that, you know, people took towards a family like that. Whether they, you know, they didn't ride them out of town on the rail or anything, they let them stay. Well, yes, because they had a right to stay if they wanted to, because they squat on a piece of ground somewhere, you know. And the family was nice, children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you're like a family that way, why are you going to help?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No. They're hungry. I haven't got now.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I was going to ask you, too, about how big an area would trade at Elmer. I mean, how far around here people would come in and use it. You said all the way out to Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean in earlier days?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, in the early days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many would you say?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Christ, I don't know. I just... I mean when you were growing up there. I don't know that part of the world.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you say, Floyd? They'd come in from as far as Park, right?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No. And from that way. Well, my dad made quite a few come from the ridge up there in Trinity Theater, up and down that farming country.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, yes, they'd have to come this way from Park or go to work. Kendrick wouldn't they, to do their shopping and trading.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: But they was just like we did. We laid in a year's supply. And we could get it, you know, when it was like that. And that way they didn't have to go to town for it, you know, just maybe a couple times a year, that is, for food.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you lay in your supply at? Where did you get your supply at for food? I mean, did you go to Troy or Moscow or to lay in? You made our folks to the store?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, the railroad is here then. And my dad, they could order from Spokane and get their supplies from there, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then was that Rama grocery?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: After the railroad went in, then it was easy to get the supplies for their stores. Well, my dad, I don't remember when I was a kid, he'd done all the trading from Troy. But there wasn't no railroad in there then. Well, he was taking me along with him. He'd tell us early in the morning and it'd be dark when we got back at night.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, after the railroad went in, it sure helped everybody. They could get more jobs and then they could, you know, the stores were better, the two stores that was right here. Well, this country around here wasn't even long. There was quite a lot of work. They hired a lot of men to work in the woods. It was a good place to live.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you and Troy go together for very long before you got married? Oh, I imagined a couple of years, didn't we?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I guess so. We kind of liked each other from time to small.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? When you were kids, you liked each other?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, no. My mother would let me go with the boys until I was, oh, I imagine, 14. Then I didn't get to go in just a little party, you know, just right here in the... I'll tell you something. They had a dance, the schoolhouse, one night. And we called it, their folks were not them dance. And so I had another boy come and get the girls. Her dad was sitting in the living room. I don't think he even spoke to us when I come in the house. And he had the newspaper and he had it sitting there, and he had it all spread out like that. And this darn kid was with me. He'd go over there and read that paper and run back to his chair again. And he just went through that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Don't you think my dad didn't know it?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He knew he was doing it. He don't know it, but he's about half mad for the girls. And so we went to dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't take the girls?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, yes. They did. They had to climb an archen before they left. I bet all the time, Pop was sitting with that paper. He was grinning behind it because you don't think he didn't know that Herb was doing that. That Herb was... That was my sister's boyfriend that come after Herb.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was reading the paper?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, he'd just slip up like that, like he was reading the paper. I don't read your show from me there all the time. He was a regular cut -up. My dad did pull that paper down about that time. I bet you he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now why didn't you want the girls to go to the dance?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He didn't want us to dance. He didn't want us to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it religious?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, to a certain, not fanatics, but they just didn't believe in dancing, and they didn't believe in playing cards, and they didn't believe in drinking. My mother didn't. They were just a little strict. They didn't hurt us any. Grandpa believed in drinking. Grandpa believed in drinking, though. Well, he drank, yes, some. Not too much in the later years he didn't. But he did. So he did let you go anyway. Oh, yeah. Even though they were sort of against him. He didn't tell us we couldn't go, you know. He just didn't want us to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't know, I guess he was about 16, 17, wasn't he?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, no, no. And we were just going to... Well, that was over to one of the neighbors. Well, when you were going together before you got married, what was the kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else could kids do for a good time besides go into a dance? Was there any other places where you could go together?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, we did. They had literary, you know, and entertainment at the schoolhouse, and Sunday school, and then had little parties. And then we'd go out for drive, you know, go to theory, after they put in the theater. Well, we went to the show there, you know. Oh, we had entertainment, but it was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to the movies when you were dating?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get married right around here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: We was married in Moscow.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I'm really curious about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get shivareed after you got married?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. They didn't do it well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They didn't know it for a day, did they?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, you weren't on the phone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? Where?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I don't know. That's me. Uh -huh. Uh -huh. Well, we get down there. And they... Well, let's see. All the rest of the crew, and I didn't. I didn't pick out the horses. Well, we gotta go over that, and see her. They made you up. Well, they'll figure that out after we get down there, because we don't really want the candy yet. But we...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you guys have much of an idea of what these old -time lumberjacks have?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: You worked with them. They were really different than... Well, sir, you know, that's the honest bunch of men that I've ever met. Well, we'll... If we take the stuff that you have to stay there and go over that in the morning, and then she may go home. And some of them old guys will buy $600. They let them have that. I wonder if I've got her a moon number.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can I get them?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah. Some old guys are not over there. Oh, I'm not... This is what we have. Oh, yes it is. Yes, it is. They have... They have to get the darn aggravated atom that's cast. No, they have to get it. They'll come back and move away. Yeah, you can tell if in a storm you get real quick milk or it comes down in the morning. I guess you had to watch them keep the caps in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had to do what?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Watch them keep the catacombs running over the track. The carriage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: They put down the... Well, it's just fumbling down. And she fell and was always coming down.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I know this old guy called him Flee Hall Brown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are you getting hungry?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, no, I'm getting hungry. I'm trying to get something. I didn't hire no outside men. That girl picked me off when I got started. And we come in with that speeder and I bet there'd be a hundred men locked to that speeder. And we'll know when they can go back to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would this be now?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, you'd go to camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, at camp six in me or something?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, over on T And then we'd come back in and send men a disc law firm last year. And they'd want to know from us when they go to work. And of course we didn't. I finally got to tell them, I said, well, you'll leave the office and they'll hear names on the book. They're going out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they say, yeah, you know, why you ain't going out?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: There's an old guy that worked for the company. Well, I just practiced all his life. And he rode in there with us. And they weren't supposed to even feed him. They didn't say nothing. They didn't eat. And I took my lunch out. I took his lunch out for two weeks. He'd go out and stay out in the timber there all day. He might give me his lunch. But they finally put him back on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was Sleigh Hall Brown? Or was this not Sleigh Hall Brown?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I heard he worked till he was real old. Yeah, he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that's when they laid him off, huh?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I don't know. They didn't lay him off because he was real old when he went in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would the crews come from? Where would you get them?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, they come all over the country. There's a lot of local guys. And then there's a lot of regular lumberjacks that stay around Spokane. That's where they come from.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I know my brother or my son are out pet down southern Idaho. I said, come on, by God, Keith, I don't see a lumberjack in that bunch. No, he said they're not all farmers. They were all distribution homes. One night he was coming in from work and went down in front of his crew. And he said, well, if I was here with my dad, he said, you know, he knows more. He's forgotten more about logging than I ever knew. And it would be cheaper than I'm thinking. I know. I've got an uncle. I don't know what camp it was, but he talks about a long time ago. And Daniel, they ever went up in there, was from Finland. I was an elk or when they said there's going to be some spurs in there. And they got a whole, I don't know, they must have been 50 then guys. One of them come in our bunkhouse and the guys, they'd come in there at night and you couldn't, you just want to get up on your boat and lay down. You couldn't tell what they'd say. And they'd all talk to him, talking to Finland. And the guy had a swamp for me. He said, different than them Finns off when they come in there. And I said, all right. He's a big Tuskegee guy. And I come in, this Finland is in there. He said, now listen, next time them friends of yours come in here, you talk American or we're going to throw everything out. He managed to. But they come in, but they talk American after that.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I'm going to be getting going. I just wanted to ask you about your chivalry though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember it still?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh yes, there was a bunch of the kids come down and they banged around and cowbells and tin cans. And so we had them come in and then we had some cookies and coffee and stuff, you know, whatever they wanted to drink. They stayed a little while and left.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Sounds like you got all easy. Well, we did. We sure must have had a little treat of some kind, candy and cigars and things, you know, the ones that smoked. But, oh, we had good times. We had the, oh, you know, the neighbors that have parties and they're once a week or once in two weeks, you know. Somebody had a birthday and had a big party and they had to play games until it got, you know, later years and then we just went. We went out and used the cars and just like we do now. But we had quite a time. Everybody did. Thank you, Coach. You know, I think we had a better time than we have. We went over to your house at Park, over to Schoolhouse at Park one night to Dan. That's after we was married. That was 1930. Yeah, that was the night that that thing went through the sky. Yeah. That's when he went through the sky and I don't know where it went, but saying it sure lightened up everything, I'll tell you that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a meter?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, a meter. Yeah. That.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah. We went all the way to Park.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go too?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, I had my car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just for dance, huh?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, they used to have lots of them. Yeah. One house in the dance and the other in the nation. You can't go from here to the house. That would be much more awesome.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did that change very much once your pavilion got going?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Did most of the dancers, I mean, I imagine they didn't have as many country dances as they had. Well, I don't know. We didn't run that in the wintertime.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: People just, I know we put on a fourth of the gas operation up here. And they had one in Lewiston. Boy, they've been this businessman, I imagine. They all owned his heart and they all want to get out of town. They all come up here. Well, I was going to ask you one thing about that Bowery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who built those Bowerys? Well, didn't your dad build that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah. Well, they just had a little celebration up there. They're the most favorite. They're the most better neighbors and they had a big time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your favorite communities that had the celebration to build one in Bowery?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, just a little place to dance and get together, you know. Just a friend. It wasn't outside people at all. It was just the neighbors. And just to have a little get together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big would you say they were? I mean, as far as how much room would there be inside one?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I imagine they'd be about very inside. I suppose they figured on the big crowd there. But the one, I remember my dad built down there. I had figures about 30 to 40. Yeah, it wasn't too big. Because there wasn't too many to come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a rectangle then? Or was it a sort of circular?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, it was a square. Just a floor, you see, with poles up and the cover over it. Just for that time of the year. It wasn't built for winter or anything like that. But just for the little celebration they'd get together and have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd tear it down?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, they'd tear it down later on. Now, Martin Anderson, we built one down there this side of Kennedy Ford. And by golly, he was going to the good that he ordered his up there in the wintertime. Yeah, he made a regular haul or a ribbon out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just with the bowels on top and everything?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, no, he put a roof on us. Yeah, he finally is going to good. He just started there, you know. But he just made a regular haul out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, is that the one that they had right by Kennedy Ford? They used to get flooded out by the river? It started out as a bowery?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, that one was Martin Anderson. And Martin Anderson had it started out as a bowery. Didn't K.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wire Hanson build one down there? Who?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: K. Wire Hanson.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he build one down there, kind of around like yours?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I don't know. He built his four -wheel -builders. Yeah, that's what I'm saying. That's the still other stuff. There was still standing that little old bowery on top of the hill when we moved up here. When your dad built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, well, did they have one year after year?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, no, I don't think so. Just be, if they decided to have one, build one, they'd build it for the summer, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would people picnic in there?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah, just get together, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go to revival around here?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, we went. What were they like when you were... Well, I don't know. They wasn't too severe because, of course, I don't know what you called.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Oh, yeah, the men... And the Pharaoh had him with him several times. And he had a dog with him. Fred Herrick had a dog with him, but I never did know if Dick Ferrell would be in blind. It might have been Dick instead of Herrick. Yeah, but Fred was with Dick. Fred Herrick was with Dick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his preaching like?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He was a very good preacher. You see, he was a pug to start with, you know. He was a boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the men, how many of the men listened to him?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: There'll be a lot of them. You'd be surprised. You'd be surprised if the 10 more members actually go with their enemies, please. That cook chatted, he'd just put in your clothes. You know, I sure did buy a little three -cornered skillet. I did, too, a lot. But I want to put that gravy in. You take one step, old guys. They wasn't religious. No, but they'd go listen to him. Got a couple of tips.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The gravy in this one?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Was he a kind of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he like a fire and brimstone kind of preacher?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Not too bad. No, he wasn't. He wasn't. You can imagine that you think he wasn't, he wasn't. No, he was a pretty calm preacher. I thought he was in the way. He's bad. He's bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you fellas know Malchor Anderson?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I worked for him. We both worked for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Malchor as a foreman?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He was the best foreman that there were at. That's right. He did it much easier in the rest of the living room. He wasn't no comparison. No. I'll tell you another reason, I heard... He was along with Uncle. Yeah, he was his uncle. He was running the Camp 37 over there at the Mission Medics. I don't know. I was up here for the first time. But I didn't know about it. So, Naughton and Tom Kenny come into camp. Malchor was drunk.

speaker: Speaker 2
words: Naughton was drunk. I'm going to camp him.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Tom Kenny was a super over Naughton. He said to Tom Kenny, he said, Mr. Naughton, just do that man alone. He said, if any kind of be done around here, he'll do it. He said, if that man's getting more logs drunk, the rest of them can't solve it. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's it mean to that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He just had an act of laying out his work and everything just went so darn smooth. Well, he fixed it. He fixed up... He had everything fixed up so he couldn't. When you went on the steps, it was all skidded up and you ran on the steps. You could start right in the hall and logs as soon as you got there.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: But he was a good guy to work for. I wonder what made him so good. He just seemed to... He knew... He planned it better. He was just a better roader. He was just a natural born... His brother was a walker, but he was much better than actual. There was no comparison.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he cared about the men?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yes, he only really cared about the men. You bet. You tell me up the dill curve there, he said, they cut us down. He said, I want the men now. He said, they're going to allow you to make so much a day. He said, I'll give you the same money as you're getting. It's getting... As you're getting now. He said, just go out and take it easy. He said, when you get $5 worth in, sit down, we won't do no more. He said, you know, I had to put it right down before they had to just work as hard as I could to make it. They said they wouldn't quit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said I had to what?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He had to... The war of the wages so they had to work real hard to make it. He was actually on the strip. He was getting his rolls cheap enough that he wouldn't have cut on the dill if they had just used your head. I don't want to hurt him. He put me on the strip and... old Buck had it. He gave in pretty good money. He said, I want to give you some money to get a buck. He was a pretty good timber. I put him a few rolls. He said, don't get... Don't put in too much. He said, you're just loud so much. He said, when you get that in, just put that down on the hill and set that. It's pretty good timber and that voting group can down, vote them down every night. I kept on the guy and... I did come back. I liked it. I made big money there, but I made them do it. It sounds like...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would he have minded if you'd have put in more?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah. He wasn't any goody when the guy... They were just paying so much and that was it. Oh, I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was wage, right?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, that wage. He had a wage and he had a scale set that was important to make so much. When that was made, that was it. Well, it sounds like maybe it would Axel have wanted you to keep working after you'd made the scale. No, he wasn't. He was nothing. I put in a strip over there and I went back and the guy, the swamper, he went and got his check and I didn't die. Come on home and I got my money back. He said, holy Jesus, boy. He said, you made more money than I made last month. I said, my God, I worked and earned it too. But that didn't make no difference. He's tried to get even with me ever since. Well, that's the time I was over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you mentioned that Pusco, did you say it was deep Pusco?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He saw you working out there and you weren't getting no logs out of it because it was the bad conditions. Well, in the summer before, all we had was the back end of the ship. So he was all along the whole. He didn't raise it any of the bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, you want me to get out now?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I said, well, you guarantee me wages are going. No, I said, I won't do that for once. Then I ain't going then. Then when he figured out I was going, I was going to take my mortgage back. He said, well, I'll grant you wages.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea what happened with Melter, why he killed himself?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I don't have any idea. He just drank so much that he just... Well, I'll tell you what happened. He come in, he finished up the seamos. My brother was in the camp. He put up the camp for him and put Melter up there. Well, then they were moving the process and moving down to camp 39. That's not getting the white line. Well, Melter come into the car seat and he did his pickup. And they couldn't find the darn pickup. And so, Parker was super, so... I guess that when Parker got a hold of it, Parker took the pickup work. But then when he sobered up, I didn't follow the name of Albert Johnson. When he was up there, we was in the process of moving up there to the Golden Railroad, right in to camp 43. Well, Melter, he come along and was talking to us. Well, he says, I'm going to see you boys in the morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, are you out?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He says, I'm going to be in the house with the grade balls. We were sitting there talking, and it was time for him to get to Albert and I to eat. And I was like, well, let's back up what we were doing. We were setting the toilet there in this house, in this area. It was an office building, and we were making a buck out there. We were in the process of setting the toilet there. He was putting the toilet in the... And when we got done, we just went down and set out there in the sunny... Well, it was the first of October, but it was a nice day. And we were stepping up there in front, and we were waiting for lunchtime, too. And Melter got up and says, well, I'll see you boys in the morning. He says, I'm going to be Mallory's grade boss. And by God, you know, we shot ourselves out of the way.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: When I went down there, we were going down.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: But I'll tell you, when Melter went on the bunk, that was it. Yeah, he stayed there. He got over it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that take a long time?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, it was about a week in Helper. I mean, it was about a week in Parkview. In the meantime, they'd hired another man to take camp 39.

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He'd done the same thing in the 1940s and 34s when they had that big fire over there in the P -10. And he was running the trench and the leaf camp up here for the Forest Service. He had two assistants in the clerk. And that part of it got so big that they sent Malchor over there. And Malchor, he was over the whole darn works. He was over the whole works. And by golly, when he was over there, he took that Forest Service pickup and pulled in the kooky and hit the dang thing and went on a great big drunk. Well, of course, that ended him with a poison. That was right there in the fire. Yeah, you bet. You bet. They put Malchor over there, the whole dang business over there. He was over at least 5 ,000 men. He was over 5 ,000 men. Well, I wouldn't be excited at him anything. No, you bet not. But like I say, when it was all over, then in the kooky, he went and hit his pickup.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When the fire was over?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah, when the fire was over, you bet. So he didn't quit until the job was done. He never went and drunk until the job was done. Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Man, you wanted to watch out?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: He didn't camp, Carl. He didn't camp. Oh, yeah, but I mean, yeah, but I mean in camp. He would never see him. I know Nargo was over there until three times. I know, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where'd you know him?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I said, I don't know. I did. But I mean, it was like now when they moved camp 40. He picked up that time in the same way. Yeah, sure. He was drunk over there at camp 37. I know that. That's another one of the canning that time. But this was a, yeah, this was a big, something big, like fire. Yeah, you bet. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get mean when he got drunk?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, no, no, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: You bet. Yeah. In fact, you couldn't hardly tell it on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That he was drunk?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Yeah. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you talked to him that day before he shot himself, did you think he was feeling real low?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: No, he wasn't. No, because he, that's the last thing he said to Albert Johnson. Now he says, well, I'll see you boys in the morning. He says that I'm going to be Malory's great boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he drinking that night before he killed himself?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I don't have no idea. See, I can help. I wasn't an Albert. Bet. He was a wonderful man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there, did many, did he have a big funeral?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: I guess so. I didn't get a chance to go. Albert went, so consequently Albert was a fall bearer, and I wasn't. So I had to be there to get the cats going in the morning, get water in them and one thing or another. So I didn't get a chance to go. Albert Johnson went. We was working together. And so I stayed, and Albert was a fall bearer, and I wasn't. So...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's that?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Well, I'm going to get going. See how I'm going to get going towards your dinner. I hope you don't mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I stayed so long again, but why?

speaker: Floyd Lawrence or Nola Lawrence or Carl Lancaster or Laura May Lancaster
words: Because I'm going to get going.
